narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.40 and a middle-class family background, you've put real effort into building your profile for law school, including planning to attend full-time. The admissions model we use predicted a 41% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which is above our threshold for approval, leading to the tough decision to reject your application right now. This prediction isn't a judgment on your potential or character—it's based on patterns from thousands of past students' academic records, test scores, and backgrounds compared to how they performed on the bar. The biggest factor pulling against you was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st, which is quite low compared to the typical student's 5th or 6th decile position; this stood out as the strongest signal pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA was a standout strength, well above the average of 3.21, and it had the most significant positive influence, strongly nudging the prediction toward you passing the bar. Your middle family income level also helped a bit, aligning with averages and contributing mildly in your favor, as did your full-time enrollment plan, which is the norm for most students. Less influential but still noted were your Year 1 decile of 1st, similar to Year 3 and below average, and your LSAT score of 20, which is notably lower than the typical 37—both of these slightly tipped toward failure risk. Overall, while your GPA gives you a real edge over many applicants, the low decile rankings in your early school years carried more weight in this model's eyes, placing you outside the profile of students who typically pass the bar first time. Compared to average applicants, you're stronger in GPA but weaker in school deciles and LSAT, which shifted the balance. I know this stings, especially after your hard work, but it's not the end of the road—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades to improve those deciles, or gaining more academic experience to reshape your profile. You have the GPA foundation to build on, and many students in similar spots succeed by addressing these gaps. Reach out to admissions advisors for personalized strategies, and keep pushing—you've got strengths that shine through. We're rooting for your next steps.
